Summary of what was found: FedEx states parcel charges are based on the greater of dimensional weight or actual weight, so material weight reduction alone does not always reduce parcel cost.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: actual weight versus dimensional weight in parcel shipping. Packaging/material relevance: any beauty shipper, mailer, carton, or e-commerce outer pack. Actual versus dimensional relevance: FedEx defines dimensional weight as the space a package occupies relative to actual weight and says billing is based on whichever is greater. Cost/emissions/damage implication: a lighter package may not reduce parcel spend if the pack still bills on dimensional weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: apply this as a screening rule before claiming freight savings from lighter materials; determine whether each SKU is actual-weight-billed or dimensional-weight-billed. Limitation: carrier-specific parcel logic, not a universal all-mode freight rule.

Summary of what was found: DHL eCommerce says parcel chargeable weight is based on the greater of total actual weight and total dimensional weight.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: parcel/e-commerce chargeable weight. Packaging/material relevance: cosmetics and personal care mailers, cartons, and tertiary packaging used in D2C shipping. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL instructs shippers to calculate dimensional weight from package dimensions and compare it to actual weight; invoice charges are based on the greater value. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter materials can reduce shipping cost only when actual weight remains the billing driver or when material changes affect pack density enough to change chargeable weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: treat parcel SKUs as separate from pallet freight and avoid assuming every gram saved converts into carrier savings. Limitation: formula and divisor are carrier-program-specific.
Source name: Chargeable Shipping Weight and Cost — DHL eCommerce — https://www.dhl.com/us-en/home/ecommerce/business-help-center/chargeable-weight.html

Summary of what was found: DHL Global Forwarding shows chargeable weight differs by mode: air uses volumetric conversion, ocean LCL uses volume, rail uses another density ratio, and road varies by carrier/location.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: multimodal freight charging logic. Packaging/material relevance: international cosmetics supply chains moving components, finished goods, and e-commerce replenishment by air, ocean, rail, or road. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL lists volumetric-weight formulas for air, ocean LCL, and rail, and notes road can vary case by case. Cost/emissions/damage implication: material-based lightweighting has the clearest shipping-cost benefit where freight is weight-constrained; where freight is cube/volume-priced, logistics benefits may depend more on density/loading efficiency than grams alone. Use in lightweighting evaluation: test each packaging route against its real transport mode and billing basis before monetizing savings. Limitation: formulas are directional education guidance, not negotiated customer tariffs.
Source name: Calculating Chargeable Weight by Air, Ocean, Road and Rail — DHL Global Forwarding — https://www.dhl.com/in-en/home/global-forwarding/freight-forwarding-education-center/calculating-chargeable-weights.html

Summary of what was found: ISTA guidance says sustainable packaging should aim for optimum protection with minimum packaging and describes a reduce-to-damage approach.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: trade-off between reducing packaging and preserving distribution performance. Packaging/material relevance: primary, secondary, and tertiary packs for fragile beauty items such as glass bottles, droppers, and premium sets. Actual versus dimensional relevance: not a billing source, but essential for interpreting whether weight reduction is economically safe. Cost/emissions/damage implication: reducing packaging without validating protection can raise damage, returns, and replacement freight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use this as a methodological principle for material-downgauge or material-substitution recommendations. Limitation: testing philosophy source, not product-category-specific pass/fail thresholds for cosmetics.
Source name: Guidelines for Selecting and Using ISTA Test Procedures — International Safe Transit Association — https://ista.org/docs/ISTA_2017_Guidelines.pdf

Summary of what was found: ISTA says 3-Series and retailer/carrier-specific 6-Series tests simulate transport hazards across parcel, LTL, truckload, retail, and e-commerce systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: protective-packaging performance under real distribution hazards. Packaging/material relevance: lighter corrugated shippers, inserts, trays, paper void fill, air pillows, and alternative protective formats for beauty products. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; testing determines whether lighter material solutions remain viable through parcel and retail distribution even if they reduce weight. Cost/emissions/damage implication: logistics economics must include hazard exposure and damage probability, not only freight weight. Use in lightweighting evaluation: require transport-test evidence for fragile or premium cosmetic packs, especially those moving through parcel or e-commerce channels. Limitation: ISTA describes test families and use cases, but detailed protocols may require separate access.
Source name: Test Procedures — International Safe Transit Association — https://ista.org/test_procedures.php

Summary of what was found: Amazon’s Ships in Product Packaging program can reduce added packaging and fulfillment fees but requires product testing; fragile, liquid, or sharp items may need ISTA 6 lab testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: e-commerce fulfillment cost, packaging minimization, and protection validation. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce products including liquids, breakables, and premium presentation packs. Actual versus dimensional relevance: not a carrier-rate page, but links packaging design to fulfillment cost and truck reduction. Cost/emissions/damage implication: eliminating added packaging can reduce fulfillment costs and carbon footprint, but testing is mandatory for higher-risk products. Use in lightweighting evaluation: e-commerce-ready packs must pass channel-specific performance tests before logistics savings are counted. Limitation: Amazon fulfillment program, not a universal retail standard.

Summary of what was found: Pregis states damaged products can cost retailers millions, and a single return can incur hundreds of dollars when all functions are counted.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: hidden cost of damage and returns. Packaging/material relevance: fragile cosmetics, glass bottles, gift sets, and e-commerce parcel shipments where under-protection can negate material savings. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; freight savings cannot be evaluated separately from damage economics. Cost/emissions/damage implication: if lighter materials reduce protective performance, replacement shipments, reverse logistics, labor, and lost sales can overwhelm packaging-weight savings. Use in lightweighting evaluation: assess lightweighting options on total delivered-cost risk, not only pack material cost or freight cost. Limitation: supplier-published directional figures rather than category-specific cosmetics benchmarks.
Source name: How Damaged Product Impacts Your Profitability — Pregis — https://www.pregis.com/globalassets/white-papers-and-case-studies/2019---updated/white-paper-6-part-analysis-damaged-product-impact-2019.pdf

Summary of what was found: NRF and Appriss reported $743 billion of merchandise returned in 2023, providing context for the scale of returns as a retail cost burden.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: retail returns as a cost burden. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce because packaging-related damage contributes to the broader returns cost environment. Actual versus dimensional relevance: none directly; contextual evidence only. Cost/emissions/damage implication: returns create additional handling, processing, transport, and loss costs. Use in lightweighting evaluation: background evidence for why avoiding avoidable damage-related returns matters; pair with damage-specific sources rather than using alone. Limitation: not all returns are packaging damage and the article focuses partly on return fraud.
Source name: NRF and Appriss Retail Report: $743 Billion in Merchandise Returned in 2023 — National Retail Federation — https://nrf.com/media-center/press-releases/nrf-and-appriss-retail-report-743-billion-merchandise-returned-2023

Summary of what was found: DHL says lightweight materials can lower shipping costs and sustainable packaging can reduce product damage, warehouse clutter, shipping weights, and long-term logistics costs.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: business case linking packaging choices to shipping cost, space use, and damage. Packaging/material relevance: paper, recycled-content, reusable, and minimalist tertiary/e-commerce formats. Actual versus dimensional relevance: DHL notes lighter materials reduce shipping costs while also linking savings to shipping volume and operational efficiency, reinforcing that weight is only one part of transport economics. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter materials can reduce fees and emissions, but protection and scalability still need validation. Use in lightweighting evaluation: general logistics-sector support that packaging-weight reductions can matter commercially. Limitation: high-level guidance rather than SKU-level cost modeling.
Source name: Sustainable Packaging: 8 Examples & Solutions for You — DHL — https://www.dhl.com/discover/en-global/logistics-advice/sustainability-and-green-logistics/sustainable-packaging-in-logistics

Summary of what was found: Mondi states lighter, well-protected e-commerce packaging using recycled and fresh paper can reduce waste and last-mile carbon emissions.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: last-mile transport efficiency and emissions. Packaging/material relevance: beauty e-commerce shippers, paper mailers, corrugated packs, and fresh-fibre/recycled-fibre paper solutions. Actual versus dimensional relevance: the source mixes lighter and more compact packs; for this report only the material-weight portion should be carried forward. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter paper-based transport packaging can reduce emissions, but Mondi ties this to adequate strength and protection rather than weight reduction alone. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use for paper-based tertiary packaging routes while separating valid material-downgauge options from out-of-scope size redesign. Limitation: statement mixes weight and compactness.
Source name: The last mile in eCommerce — Mondi — https://www.mondigroup.com/news-and-insight/2024/the-last-mile-unpacked-driving-ecommerce-with-sustainable-packaging/

Summary of what was found: International Paper says corrugated inserts and specialty packaging can replace plastic foam and loose-fill for damage-prone liquid-filled plastic and glass bottles.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: protective-packaging substitution for fragile bottles in transit. Packaging/material relevance: cosmetics and personal care because the source specifically names liquid-filled plastic and glass bottles and identifies corrugated inserts as alternatives to foam and peanuts. Actual versus dimensional relevance: indirect; replacing heavier or more material-intensive protective formats may improve recyclability and potentially reduce overall shipper material use. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports paper-based protective inserts for fragile liquid products, but protection must remain adequate. Use in lightweighting evaluation: evidence that corrugated inserts are a credible tertiary/secondary protective route for bottle-based beauty SKUs. Limitation: no quantified cost or weight delta.
Source name: Paper-based Recyclable Packaging Fuels Design Innovations — International Paper — https://www.internationalpaper.com/resources/recycling/article/paper-based-recyclable-packaging-fuels-design-innovations

Summary of what was found: Pregis says its air-cushion system is more than 99% air and can reduce packaging material, transport costs, storage space, and return-related impacts.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: lightweight protective packaging for parcels. Packaging/material relevance: tertiary packaging choices such as air pillows for void fill and cushioning in beauty e-commerce shipments. Actual versus dimensional relevance: actual-weight advantage is strong because the protective medium is mostly air; dimensional billing may still limit savings for oversized cartons. Cost/emissions/damage implication: air pillows are attractive where lightweight protection matters and where storage-space reductions matter. Use in lightweighting evaluation: treat air cushions as a baseline lightweight protective option against which paper void fill, molded fibre, and corrugated inserts can be compared. Limitation: recyclability and curbside acceptance depend on local systems and film type; source is supplier-authored.
Source name: Eco Friendly Air Cushions AirSpeed — Pregis — https://uk.pregis.com/globalassets/resources/eu/sustainability/sustai~1.pdf

Summary of what was found: Sealed Air says kraft paper mailers weigh less and generate less solid waste than corrugated boxes, giving postage savings, and are made with 100% recycled paper fibers.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: tertiary-pack choice between mailers and boxes. Packaging/material relevance: lightweight, non-fragile beauty items and flat/soft goods where a paper mailer can replace a corrugated box. Actual versus dimensional relevance: supports actual-weight and postage savings; strongest where the item can safely ship in a mailer rather than a box. Cost/emissions/damage implication: lighter tertiary material can reduce postage and waste, but only for SKUs that do not need rigid protection. Use in lightweighting evaluation: use as a material route for mass-market and refill/accessory subcategories, not for fragile glass or luxe presentation-dependent packs unless validated. Limitation: package-format suitability is product-dependent and not a universal corrugated substitute.

Summary of what was found: Smurfit says honeycomb cardboard is lightweight, fills gaps/voids, prevents movement, offers stacking strength up to 800 kPa, and can lower shipping costs without compromising stability.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: paper-based protective inserts and void-fill/stabilization. Packaging/material relevance: tertiary protective packaging and replacement of heavier protective components for premium gift sets, fragile glass, or multi-item beauty bundles. Actual versus dimensional relevance: primarily actual-weight and stability related. Cost/emissions/damage implication: honeycomb paper is a lighter protective option that still provides compression and stacking performance. Use in lightweighting evaluation: include honeycomb paperboard as a candidate to replace foam blocks, denser inserts, or multi-material protective components where compression and movement control matter. Limitation: no direct beauty-specific case or cost comparison.

Summary of what was found: Smurfit says corrugated cardboard pallets are 70% lighter than wood pallets and can reduce shipping costs, carbon emissions, and manual-handling strain.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: pallet-level tertiary packaging and handling efficiency. Packaging/material relevance: palletized bulk shipments of finished beauty goods, components, and secondary-pack assemblies. Actual versus dimensional relevance: pure actual-weight benefit at pallet/load level; pallet tare directly affects truck and air-freight payload utilization. Cost/emissions/damage implication: cutting pallet tare can reduce load weight and potentially improve manual handling and safety. Use in lightweighting evaluation: useful for tertiary-pack screening at distribution-center/export level where wood pallets are avoidable. Limitation: performance depends on load profile, moisture exposure, and export handling conditions.

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer states that in luxury cosmetics, glass weight is often perceived as a sign of high value, even though lighter glass design improves sustainability.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: premium/luxe packaging’s intentional use of high-weight primary packs. Packaging/material relevance: fragrance, skincare, and luxury beauty packs using heavy glass. Actual versus dimensional relevance: mostly actual-weight issue; heavy glass increases pack tare and transport/handling burden. Cost/emissions/damage implication: premium/luxe brands may accept higher logistics cost because weight contributes to consumer-perceived value, but lightweight glass design is increasingly considered. Use in lightweighting evaluation: supports splitting recommendations into luxe and mass-market tracks because premium packaging has a different value equation and cannot be screened on logistics cost alone. Limitation: perception/value statements are segment-specific and should not be generalized across all beauty products.
Source name: Lightweight Packaging Design for Less — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/en/customer/products-solutions/cosmetics/sustainable-packaging-design/design-for-less

Summary of what was found: Berry/Amcor says plastic’s lighter weight can reduce the number of vehicles needed, allow more bottles per truck within pack-out limits, and had a cited 15% carbon-emission advantage for a plastic soap bottle versus glass.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: primary-pack material substitution and transport efficiency. Packaging/material relevance: personal care bottles and cosmetic body-care or hair-care formats where plastic can replace glass. Actual versus dimensional relevance: clear actual-weight transport case because lighter bottles can increase payload efficiency and lower indirect transport emissions. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports glass-to-plastic substitution where brand, compatibility, and recyclability allow; also implies lower breakage risk versus glass. Use in lightweighting evaluation: evidence for evaluating glass-to-PET/HDPE/PP transitions in mass-market SKUs and selected premium-adjacent categories. Limitation: aesthetics, compatibility, and premium-brand equity may limit applicability in luxe beauty.
Source name: Plastic or Glass? Why Choose Plastic Instead of Glass — Berry / Amcor Packaging Solutions — https://packagingsolutions.amcor.com/en/news/articles/plastic-or-glass-how-do-you-decide

Summary of what was found: The Flexible Packaging Association says flexible packaging usually has lower fossil-fuel use, GHG emissions, and water use because of very light weight, efficient roll/flat shipping, and high product-to-package ratio.
Detailed description of what was found: Logistics issue covered: system-level transport and resource efficiency of flexible formats. Packaging/material relevance: refill pouches, sachets, flexible mailers, and lightweight films used in primary or tertiary beauty packaging. Actual versus dimensional relevance: strong actual-weight advantage because the format is very light and inbound materials ship flat or on rolls. Cost/emissions/damage implication: supports flexible refill pouches and film-based routes as logistics-weight reduction candidates where breakage resistance and high product-to-package ratio matter. Use in lightweighting evaluation: directional basis for comparing refill/flexible routes against rigid bottles or jars in mass-market and e-commerce scenarios. Limitation: end-of-life and recyclability constraints for flexible formats need separate screening and should not be assumed favorable in every market.
